s2.q: What is the difference between cord presentation and cord prolapse?
options: Cord presentation occurs in breech, cord prolapse in cephalic
Cord presentation: cord below presenting part with INTACT membranes. Cord prolapse: after membrane RUPTURE
Cord presentation is diagnosed by ultrasound only
There is no difference — they are the same condition
s2.explain: Cord presentation = presence of umbilical cord below the presenting part with intact membranes. Cord prolapse = cord below the presenting part after rupture of membranes.

s2.q: The incidence of this condition is highest in which population?
options: Depends on the specific condition and risk factors
s2.explain: Incidence varies by condition — each has its own epidemiology and risk profile.

options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s2.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

options: It applies only to research settings

options: A pathological deviation from normal anatomy or function
An incidental finding with no clinical significance
s2.explain: A definition describes the essential nature of a condition as a deviation from normal.

s4.q: How many risk factors for cord prolapse does the source enumerate?
s4.explain: The source lists 10 risk factors for cord prolapse: prematurity, fetal congenital abnormality, second twin, multiparity, low birth weight (< 2.5 kg), non-cephalic presentations, CPD, low-lying placenta/long cord, polyhydramnios, and macrosomia.

s4.q: Which of the following is a risk factor for cord prolapse?
s4.explain: Polyhydramnios is a risk factor for cord prolapse because excess amniotic fluid can wash the cord out when membranes rupture.

s4.q: The etiology of this condition involves:
options: A complex interplay of genetic, environmental, and multifactorial elements
s4.explain: Most obstetric conditions have multifactorial etiology.

s5.q: What clinical sign indicates fetal viability in cord prolapse?
s5.explain: If the cord is pulsating, the fetus is alive. If the cord is not pulsating, the fetus is likely dead.

s5.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s5.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s6.q: If cord prolapse is diagnosed with the fetus alive and cervix fully dilated, the management is:
options: Replace the cord and await spontaneous delivery
Forceps or vacuum delivery (expeditious vaginal delivery)
s6.explain: If the cervix is fully dilated and the fetus is alive with cord prolapse, expeditious vaginal delivery (forceps/vacuum) is appropriate.

s6.q: If cord prolapse occurs with a live fetus and an incompletely dilated cervix, the management is:
options: Pudendal block and trial of forceps
s6.explain: When cord prolapse occurs with a live fetus and the cervix is not fully dilated, immediate cesarean section is indicated.

s6.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s6.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s6.q: Which factor most influences the choice of treatment?
options: All of the above — treatment is individualized
s6.explain: Obstetric management is tailored to gestational age, maternal status, and available resources.

s6.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.
